title: Figure 1.1: AI History 1940s-50s
caption: Timeline: McCulloch-Pitts Neuron (1943), Turing Test (1950), Perceptron (1957)

title: Figure 1.2: AI History 1960s-70s
caption: Symbolic AI, Machine Learning term coined, Perceptrons book limitations

title: Figure 1.3: AI History 1980s-Present
caption: CNNs, Backpropagation, Transformers (2017), Era of LLMs (2022-Present)

title: Figure 1.4: Neural Network Training
caption: Training process with forward propagation and backpropagation
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Understand basic structure: Input layer → Hidden layer(s) → Output layer. Deep learning = neural networks with multiple hidden layers. Used for complex pattern recognition.

title: Figure 1.5: ML Lifecycle
caption: Complete ML lifecycle from problem definition to deployment

title: Figure 1.6: Data Types in ML
caption: Structured, unstructured, semi-structured, and time-series data

title: Figure 1.7: Inference Types
caption: Batch, real-time, and asynchronous inference patterns

title: AWS Global Infrastructure
caption: Exam Focus: Know the 3 levels: Regions (geographic areas), Availability Zones (isolated data centers within regions), and Edge Locations (CDN endpoints). Regions are independent; AZs within a region are connected by low-latency links.
chapter: Why Certify? An Introduction to the AWS AI Practitioner Exam

title: AWS Management Console
caption: Exam Focus: The web-based GUI for accessing AWS services. Know that IAM users sign in here. Root account should NOT be used for daily operations - create IAM users instead.
chapter: Why Certify? An Introduction to the AWS AI Practitioner Exam

title: AWS Shared Responsibility Model
caption: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, global infrastructure). Customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, applications, OS configuration, network settings, identity management).
chapter: Why Certify? An Introduction to the AWS AI Practitioner Exam

title: Figure 2.1: Text to Embeddings
caption: Converting raw text through tokenization to embedding vectors

title: Figure 2.2: Transformer Architecture
caption: Encoder/decoder blocks with multi-head self-attention
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: CRITICAL for exam: Transformers use Attention Mechanism to process entire sequences in parallel (vs RNNs sequentially). Powers LLMs like GPT, Claude. Key innovation: self-attention.

title: GAN Architecture
caption: Exam Focus: Generative Adversarial Networks: Two networks compete - Generator (creates fake data) and Discriminator (detects fakes). Used for generating realistic images, data augmentation.
chapter: AWS Fundamentals for the AI Practitioner

title: ML Model Training Process
caption: Exam Focus: Understand the iterative process: 1) Data collection/preparation, 2) Feature engineering, 3) Model selection, 4) Training, 5) Evaluation, 6) Deployment, 7) Monitoring.
chapter: AWS Fundamentals for the AI Practitioner

title: Reinforcement Learning Diagram
caption: Exam Focus: Agent takes actions in an environment → receives rewards/penalties → learns optimal policy. Use case: AWS DeepRacer (autonomous driving). Contrast with supervised/unsupervised.
chapter: AWS Fundamentals for the AI Practitioner

title: Supervised Learning Workflow
caption: Exam Focus: Supervised learning requires LABELED data (input-output pairs). Use cases: classification (predicting categories) and regression (predicting continuous values). Contrast with unsupervised learning (no labels).
chapter: AWS Fundamentals for the AI Practitioner

title: Figure 3.1: AI by Industry
caption: AI and ML applications across healthcare, finance, retail, manufacturing
chapter: Applications of AI and ML in Real-World Use Cases

title: Figure 3.2: Healthcare AI
caption: Medical imaging, drug discovery, clinical documentation
chapter: Applications of AI and ML in Real-World Use Cases

title: Figure 3.3: Financial Services AI
caption: Fraud detection, risk assessment, algorithmic trading
chapter: Applications of AI and ML in Real-World Use Cases

title: Figure 3.4: Retail AI
caption: Recommendations, demand forecasting, visual search
chapter: Applications of AI and ML in Real-World Use Cases

title: Amazon Comprehend Output
caption: Exam Focus: NLP service extracts: entities (people, places), key phrases, sentiment (positive/negative/neutral), language detection. Use cases: social media analysis, document processing.

title: Amazon Rekognition Demo
caption: Exam Focus: Computer vision service: Object detection, facial analysis, text extraction. Can detect faces, emotions, inappropriate content. Fully managed - no ML expertise needed.

title: Amazon Textract Results
caption: Exam Focus: Extracts text AND structured data from documents (forms, tables). Preserves document structure. Use cases: invoice processing, form automation, document digitization.

title: Fine-tuning Process
caption: Exam Focus: Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained FM to specific tasks with domain-specific data. Alternative to RAG. Trade-off: more effort but better performance for specialized tasks.

title: RAG Architecture
caption: Exam Focus: Retrieval Augmented Generation: Model retrieves relevant context from external knowledge base before generating response. Reduces hallucinations, keeps model current without retraining.

title: Rekognition Face Analysis
caption: Exam Focus: Returns: face location, age range, gender, emotions, facial features, accessories (glasses). Use cases: security, user verification, photo organization.

title: Figure 4.1: AWS AI/ML Service Stack
caption: Three-layer architecture: Bedrock/Q (Gen AI), AI Services (APIs), SageMaker (ML)

title: Figure 4.2: Bedrock Console
caption: Amazon Bedrock console with foundation model selection
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: Bedrock is AWS's FM platform. Provides access to multiple FMs (Claude, Llama, etc.) via single API. Serverless - no infrastructure management.

title: Figure 4.3: Bedrock Knowledge Bases
caption: RAG architecture with data ingestion and retrieval workflow
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: Knowledge Bases implement RAG. Connects FM to your data sources (S3, databases). Uses vector embeddings for semantic search.

title: Figure 4.4: Comprehend Entity Extraction
caption: Amazon Comprehend output showing entities with confidence scores

title: Figure 4.5: SageMaker Studio IDE
caption: Amazon SageMaker Studio integrated development environment
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Amazon SageMaker is the primary AWS service for ML. Know that Studio provides an integrated IDE for the entire ML lifecycle: data prep, model building, training, and deployment.

title: Amazon Rekognition Console
caption: Exam Focus: Shows confidence scores for each detected attribute. Remember: higher confidence = more reliable. Can set thresholds for what to include in results.

title: Bedrock Playground Interface
caption: Exam Focus: Playground lets you test models interactively. Can adjust temperature (creativity vs determinism) and max tokens. Good for prompt engineering experiments.

title: Figure 5.1: Model Selection Criteria
caption: Decision framework for selecting foundation models
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Consider: Task type (text gen, embeddings, image), Model size (affects quality/cost), Context window (token limit), Latency requirements, Cost per token

title: Figure 5.2: Bedrock Model Comparison
caption: Comparison of foundation models and their capabilities
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Bedrock provides automatic evaluation metrics. Can also do human evaluation. Important for comparing models before production deployment.

title: Figure 5.3: Claude 3 Haiku Details
caption: Model information showing capabilities: Agents, Code Gen, RAG

title: Figure 5.4: Prompt Components
caption: Key components: Instruction, Context, Examples, Constraints, Format
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: 4 components - 1) Instruction (what to do), 2) Context (background info), 3) Input Data (what to process), 4) Output Indicator (format expected).

title: Agent Action Groups
caption: Exam Focus: Action groups define what APIs/tools the agent can invoke. Agent uses reasoning to determine which actions to take based on user input.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Amazon Q Business
caption: Exam Focus: Amazon Q = Generative AI assistant for BUSINESS users (not developers). Connects to enterprise data (SharePoint, S3, etc.). Answers questions, generates content, summarizes documents.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Amazon Q Developer
caption: Exam Focus: Q Developer helps DEVELOPERS: code suggestions, debugging, security scanning, infrastructure as code generation. Integrated into IDE and AWS Console.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
caption: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: Prompt model to show its reasoning step-by-step. Improves accuracy on complex problems (math, logic). Keywords: 'Let's think step by step'
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Few-Shot Prompting
caption: Exam Focus: Few-shot: Provide 2-5 examples of desired input/output. Teaches model the pattern. Improves accuracy significantly over zero-shot.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Jailbreaking Attempts
caption: Exam Focus: Jailbreaking = trying to bypass safety constraints. Techniques: role-playing, hypothetical scenarios, encoding requests. Guardrails help detect and block.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Prompt Injection Attack
caption: Exam Focus: SECURITY RISK: Malicious user inserts instructions to override system prompts. Can leak system prompts or cause harmful outputs. Use input validation and guardrails.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Zero-Shot Prompting
caption: Exam Focus: Zero-shot: No examples given. Model uses pre-trained knowledge. Fastest approach but may be less accurate for complex/nuanced tasks.
chapter: Real-World AI Applications with AWS Tools

title: Figure 6.1: Bedrock Data Automation
caption: Extracting information from documents with configurable output
chapter: Generative AI Applications with RAG and Agents

title: Figure 6.2: RAG with Bedrock
caption: Complete RAG: Data ingestion → OpenSearch → Retrieve & Generate API
chapter: Generative AI Applications with RAG and Agents

title: Figure 6.3: Bedrock Agents Architecture
caption: Agents with action groups, knowledge bases, and API integrations
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Agents = autonomous AI that can: 1) Understand user requests, 2) Break down into steps, 3) Call APIs/tools, 4) Maintain conversation context. Enables complex task automation.
chapter: Generative AI Applications with RAG and Agents

title: Figure 6.4: Agent Orchestration
caption: How agents break down requests into actionable steps
chapter: Generative AI Applications with RAG and Agents

title: Figure 6.5: Multi-modal RAG
caption: RAG supporting documents, images, audio, and video inputs
chapter: Generative AI Applications with RAG and Agents

caption: Comparison: supervised small data vs self-supervised large corpus

caption: Fine-tuning, continued pre-training, and model distillation

caption: Prepare datasets, configure training, evaluate model

caption: Side-by-side comparison of the two approaches

title: Figure 7.5: Model Distillation
caption: Transferring knowledge from large teacher to small student model

title: Figure 7.6: Model Evaluation Dashboard
caption: Automated metrics and human evaluation options
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Know these metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, AUC-ROC. Confusion matrix shows TP, FP, TN, FN. Critical for understanding model performance.

caption: Using foundation models to evaluate other model outputs

caption: Comparing accuracy, robustness, toxicity metrics

caption: Configuring custom metrics, rating scales, workforce

caption: Combining automated metrics and human judgment

caption: CI/CD for ML: integration, deployment, monitoring

title: Figure 8.2: SageMaker Pipelines
caption: Workflow orchestration for data prep, training, deployment

title: Figure 8.3: Model Registry Workflow
caption: Versioning, approval workflows, deployment management

title: Figure 8.4: Model Monitor Setup
caption: Data quality, model quality, bias drift detection

title: Figure 8.5: Blue/Green Deployment
caption: Zero-downtime model updates with instant switching

title: AWS Security Services
caption: Exam Focus: Defense in depth: Shield (DDoS), WAF (web attacks), GuardDuty (threat detection), Macie (data protection), Security Hub (centralized findings).

title: Compliance Frameworks
caption: Exam Focus: Know: HIPAA (healthcare), GDPR (EU data privacy), PCI DSS (payments), SOC (financial reporting), ISO (international standards). AWS Artifact provides compliance reports.

title: Figure 9.1: Responsible AI Workflow
caption: End-to-end: Pipelines, Registry, Clarify for governance
chapter: Implementing Responsible AI with AWS Services

title: Figure 9.2: SageMaker Clarify
caption: Data quality, bias detection, model quality assessments
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Detects bias in data and models. Measures metrics like disparate impact. Generates explainability reports. Required for responsible AI compliance.
chapter: Implementing Responsible AI with AWS Services

title: Figure 9.3: Bias Detection Report
caption: Pre/post-training bias metrics across facets
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Know these: Disparate Impact (ratio of outcomes), Statistical Parity Difference, Equal Opportunity Difference. Important for fairness in AI systems.
chapter: Implementing Responsible AI with AWS Services

title: Figure 9.4: Bedrock Guardrails
caption: Content filters, denied topics, PII redaction
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: CRITICAL: Guardrails filter harmful content. Can block: hate speech, violence, PII, profanity. Also prevents prompt injection. Essential for production GenAI apps.
chapter: Implementing Responsible AI with AWS Services

title: Figure 9.5: Guardrails Testing
caption: Testing guardrails with sample prompts
chapter: Implementing Responsible AI with AWS Services

title: Figure 10.1: AI Security Threats
caption: Prompt injection, data poisoning, model extraction attacks
examFocusTip: Exam Focus: Understand scope: Consumer apps (highest risk), Enterprise apps, Internal tools. Higher scope = more security controls needed.
